titleStmt.title: Letter from Jasper Travers Wolfe to Robert Chalmers, 19 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Jasper Travers Wolfe

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from Jasper Travers Wolfe (1872-1952), a Cork based solicitor, to the under-secretary, Sir Robert Chalmers (1858-1938). Wolfe sends the letter with the intention of enclosing a reference from the Clerk of the Crown and Peace for Cork, Mr. Henry T. Wright, which backs Wolfe's own application to the office of the Crown Solicitor of Cork.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary's Office Registered Papers; CSO RP/1916/8533/1

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
19th May,1916. To:- The Under-Secretary for Ireland,
Dublin Castle. Sir, , Following my application for appointment to the Office of
Crown Solicitor for the City of Cork and West Riding;I beg to
enclose Certificate received from Mr. Henry T. Wright, Clerk of
the Crown and Peace, Cork. I as,
Sir,
Your obedient servant, J Travers Wolfe
I have the pleasure of knowing Mr
Jasper Wolfe I think since he became
a Solicitor, he is in the employment at
present of the largest practice in West Cork
including a very extensive Quarter
Sessions and Petty Sessions practice, he
is an able advocate with a thorough
knowledge of criminal law and with
a very large experience acquired in
the defence of prisoners; I have
no hesitation in saying that I
consider him preeminently
suitable for the office of Crown
Solicitor and for which office I
hardly know anybody so suitable Henry Wright Clerk of the Crown and Peace
Court House Cork May 16th 1916
